turns.text: Hello Allegra, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels... heavy, I suppose. Being thirty-four weeks along with my third, you’d think I’d feel like a pro, but this time it’s different. It’s just me and her, and I feel this immense, quiet pressure to get everything right for her, especially things that no one else seems to take as seriously as I do.

I spend so many hours walking through the woods near my home in Sterling, just talking to her. I’m fixated on this idea of her microbiome—this invisible foundation I’m building. It sounds strange to say out loud, but I’m terrified that a medical intervention, like a C-section or even just too much sterile protocol, might rob her of that initial inoculation she needs from me. I want her to have my good bugs, not the hospital’s antiseptic scent. 

When I bring these things up with my doctor—the delayed bathing, the skin-to-skin right away—she just sighs. She sees a routine, low-risk pregnancy, but I feel like I’m standing guard over a delicate biological handoff. It’s lonely, feeling like the only person who understands how vital this "seeding" process is. I just want to give her every possible advantage before she even takes her first breath.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To be honest, I expected a sort of partnership. With my first two, the appointments felt like a routine check-in, but this time, because I’m navigating this as a single parent, I walked in needing more than just a blood pressure check. I wanted a conversation about the *how*—not just the medical "is the baby healthy," but the "how do we start this life?" 

I envisioned a space where my concerns about the microbiome and the environment of the birth room would be met with curiosity. I thought we’d sit down and map out how to protect that delicate, natural transition. Instead, it’s been... fast. I’m in a gown, the Doppler is on, the numbers look good, and then I’m ushered out. It’s efficient, sure, but it feels like they’re treating the vessel and ignoring the delicate transfer happening inside.

It’s frustrating. I have to guard my words because if I push too hard, I can see that glaze-over look in their eyes. They think I’m just reading too much on the internet. But I’m not just looking for information; I’m looking for a teammate who understands that this isn’t just a "delivery"—it’s the most important biological transfer she will ever receive. It’s been much colder and more clinical than I ever hoped it would be.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and I’d call it a negative one—was how quickly I was dismissed. I think I expected that because I’m on my third, the providers would trust my instincts more, or at least treat me like an active participant rather than a series of statistics. 

Instead, there’s this wall. I remember sitting there in the exam room, trying to articulate why I was so worried about antibiotics or standard hospital soaps affecting her gut health. I wasn't being aggressive; I was just being a mother. The doctor didn't get angry, but she just gave me this polite, pitying smile. It was like I was a child asking about fairy tales. 

It was jarring. I realized then that my maternity care isn't a collaborative project; it’s a streamlined process they’ve perfected for the masses. I’m just one of many, and my desire for a "microbiologically natural" birth is seen as a quirky, inconvenient preference rather than a valid health priority. That hit me harder than I expected. I thought I’d be walking into a partnership, but I’m really just walking into a system that expects me to be silent and compliant. It makes me feel incredibly isolated in what should be such a monumental transition.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a dialogue, really. I thought we’d look at the screens together, and the doctor would explain what those shadows and lines meant for the baby’s development—maybe even how all that scans as "normal" while still leaving room for the nuances I care about. I wanted someone to say, "Look, everything is growing perfectly, let’s talk about how we can protect that growth during birth."

The reality is just... cold information packets and a hurried summary. The results come back, and the message is always, "Everything is normal, see you in four weeks." There’s no context provided for *my* specific anxieties. They check the boxes—heart rate, growth, fluid—and then the clinical encounter ends. It feels like they're reading from a script designed to shut down further inquiry. 

When I ask if these results change anything about my birth plan or my concerns about the seeding process, I’m met with a firm, "We’ll cross that bridge when we get there." It feels like they’re waiting for a crisis to actually listen to me. I don’t want to wait for a crisis. I want a conversation about how we keep her healthy from the very first second she hits the air. But they treat information like it’s just for them, not for me.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a cavernous gap, really. It’s like they’re giving me the headlines, but I’m desperately trying to read the fine print of the entire document. When I get those printouts from my blood work or the ultrasound reports, it’s all technical jargon—percentiles and abbreviations that don’t tell me anything about the *process*. 

I don't just want to know if the baby is the right size; I want to understand how the environment of the hospital—that bright, sterilized, plastic-filled room—is going to influence her, specifically. When I try to bridge that gap by asking questions, they hand me a pamphlet. The pamphlet is full of generic advice about vitamins and exercise, but it says absolutely nothing about the bacterial environment she’ll be born into. 

It feels like they’re intentionally keeping the lens narrow. If they acknowledged the complexity of what I’m asking about, they’d have to slow down, and they aren't willing to do that. So, I’m left to piece together the science on my own at night, in the quiet, and that leaves me feeling so incredibly vulnerable. I’m carrying the burden of her future health entirely on my own shoulders, and the medical system is just... looking the other way.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I honestly expected to build a relationship—someone who would recognize me when I walked in, someone who would remember the "why" behind my fears. After all, I’m growing a human being; I thought that deserved a bit of personal investment. I expected a continuity of care where I wouldn't have to re-explain my anxieties about the microbiome every single time I stepped into the clinic.

The reality has been a revolving door. I see whoever is on the rotation that day. Some are kinder than others, but it’s always a fresh start, which is exhausting. I spend half the appointment catching them up, and the other half feeling like I’m rushing to justify my concerns before they lose interest or have to move to the next room. There is zero continuity. I’m just a chart they scan for ten seconds before they walk in.

It’s especially hard as a single parent. I don’t have a partner to help me remember what was said or to advocate for me when I’m feeling overwhelmed. I’m the only one holding the threads of my story together, and without a consistent provider to help me weave them, I feel like I’m constantly dangling over a cliff. I’m not a patient to them; I’m a case to be processed.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

The emotional toll is the hardest part. Every time I walk in and see a face I don't recognize, it’s like having to perform a play for a brand-new audience, over and over again. I feel like I have to "prove" I’m not just a frantic, hormonal mother, but someone who has done her homework on microbial transfer and long-term health. It’s exhausting to constantly assert myself when I’m already so tired.

Practically, it’s a disaster for my birth plan. I try to leave notes, or hope the electronic chart reflects what I’ve discussed, but the new doctors always have a different "style." One might be dismissive, another might be impatient, and I never know which one I’ll get when I finally go into labor. It makes me feel like the effort I put into planning is just going to be erased the moment I enter the hospital. 

I’m terrified that when the big day comes, I won't have a partner in the room who truly *gets* why the immediate skin-to-skin and the avoidance of unnecessary intervention matters to me. I’ll be surrounded by strangers who might just see me as a "low-risk delivery" to be expedited, rather than a person trying to protect the beginning of a life. It makes me feel incredibly small and powerless in a system that is supposed to be supporting me.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I suppose I expected a sense of reverence—or at least, a sense of acknowledgment that this is a sacred, complex transformation. I thought my body would be treated as a home for this baby, not just a series of measurements. I expected them to talk about the physical reality of growing her, maybe acknowledging the ache in my lower back or the effort it takes to carry this weight alone, especially while working full-time.

But the reality is that my body is treated like a machine that needs to be calibrated. The exams are so mechanical. They measure the fundal height, check the position, listen to the heartbeat, and that’s it. It’s all very "clinical gaze." They don't look at *me*; they look at the metrics. When I try to talk about how I feel, or my anxieties about her environment, they steer the conversation back to the data. 

It feels like my body is being deconstructed into parts—a blood pressure reading, a weight check, a urine sample. There’s no space for the *experience* of being pregnant, or for the bond I’m trying to cultivate. It makes me feel disconnected from my own pregnancy, like I’m an observer in my own care. They have the power, the equipment, and the authority, and I’m just the host of the guest they’re actually interested in.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one time, a few weeks ago, that still makes my skin crawl. It was a standard pelvic check—though honestly, it felt entirely unnecessary at that stage. The doctor didn’t even look me in the eye when she entered the room. She just told me to "scoot down" as if I were a piece of equipment being prepped for maintenance. 

There was no warmth, no "Are you comfortable?" or "Is it okay if we do this now?" It was just this clinical, hurried motion. While she was doing it, she was talking to a student or a nurse—I couldn’t even tell—about her lunch plans, like I wasn't even a person in the room. I felt so exposed and small. I wanted to scream, "This is my body, and that is my daughter's first home you’re poking at," but I just stayed silent because I felt like if I made a fuss, I’d be labeled the 'difficult' patient, and I didn't want that on my chart. 

I left that office feeling violated, honestly. It wasn't just physical; it was the total erasure of my autonomy. I’m carrying all this fear about the sterility of the hospital environment, and that experience just confirmed that to them, I’m just a cavity to be checked, not a mother-to-be whose boundaries and feelings matter.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven’t had to deal with a diagnosis like that, thankfully. I’m categorized as "low-risk," which is a blessing, I know. But there’s a strange irony in that label. Because I’m "low-risk," the system acts like I don’t *need* any extra time or support. 

I imagine if I *did* have something like gestational diabetes, I’d be forced into a much more rigid box, and I think that would break me. Even without a formal condition, I feel like I’m already fighting a different kind of "risk"—the risk of losing that vital microbial transfer, the risk of a sterile start, the risk of having my birth plan ignored. 

I’ve seen friends go through those higher-risk protocols, and it seems like they’re buried under so much monitoring that the "person" part of the pregnancy gets lost entirely. It’s like the medical system demands total surrender in exchange for safety. I’m already feeling that pressure to surrender, even with a clean bill of health. If I were actually dealing with a complication, I suspect I’d lose my voice completely. The system is designed to manage the biology, not the mother, and that's a terrifying thought when you’re doing this all on your own.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything feels missing, in a way. If I had to pick, I’d say it’s the lack of *shared humanity*. 

Practically, I’m drowning in the details of being a single mother—balancing work, getting the house ready, and managing the physical toll of this third pregnancy—and the medical team never asks about that. They don't offer resources for the "whole" person. I’m not just a womb; I’m a person navigating a really big life change. 

Emotionally, I am desperate for someone to just say, "I hear you, and that’s a beautiful, important goal." Instead, they treat my focus on the microbiome like a frantic hobby. I needed them to acknowledge that my anxiety comes from a place of love and a desire to give my daughter the best start, not from a place of ignorance. 

What's really missing is an invitation to collaborate. They have the medical training, but I have the intuition and the life experience of being *her* mother. If they could just bridge that gap—if they could show a little curiosity about the "seeding" process I’m so invested in—I wouldn't feel so alone. I wouldn't be walking through these woods at night feeling like I have to fight a silent war for my daughter's health before she’s even born. I just want to feel like a teammate, not an obstacle.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a direct line—a sense that if I had a worry, I could get a straight answer without jumping through a dozen bureaucratic hoops. I thought that in a modern medical system, a pregnant woman wouldn’t have to play phone tag with an automated system or wait twenty-four hours for a nurse to call back about something that feels like an emergency to her, even if it’s "routine" to them.

The reality has been incredibly frustrating. It’s always the same: "Leave a message, and we’ll get back to you by the end of the day." When you’re a single mom working full-time, "the end of the day" doesn’t work. My anxiety spikes when I’m at my desk, and I’m staring at my phone, waiting for a call that often doesn't come until I’m finally home, exhausted, and trying to focus on my other children.

It feels like they are guarding their time, not serving mine. When I finally *do* get through, the response is almost always, "If you’re worried, just go to the ER." But that’s the exact opposite of what I want! That’s the epicenter of the sterile, fluorescent world I’m trying to avoid for my daughter’s first moments. I’m not looking for an emergency room; I’m looking for a partner who cares enough to talk me down or walk me through my concerns. It’s a very lonely feeling to know that the help I’m paying for is only available if I’m willing to enter the very environment I fear most.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Every single time. It’s almost a daily calculation—*is this worth the eye-roll? Is this worth the twenty-minute hold time followed by a hurried nurse who clearly wants to go home?* 

There was a night last week where I had some strange, sharp pains, not quite contractions but definitely something different. I sat on my bed for two hours, just breathing, talking myself out of calling the on-call line. I knew exactly how it would go: they’d tell me, "If you're worried, come in for monitoring." And the thought of going to the hospital—of being put on a machine that would track my every twitch, surrounded by that clinical, sterile air—felt like a nightmare. I was terrified that if I walked through those doors, I wouldn’t leave until they had "managed" me, potentially stripping away the autonomy I have left. 

So, I stayed home. I drank water, I changed positions, I talked to her. I managed it myself because the barrier to getting "help" felt higher than the risk of just waiting it out. It’s a sad state of affairs when a pregnant woman feels safer suffering in silence than engaging with the people who are supposedly there to protect her. I don’t want to be the "difficult" patient who "cries wolf." I just want to feel heard, but I’ve learned that the price of being heard is far too high.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected my village to be there, especially this time around. I thought that by now, I’d have a rhythm with friends or family who would understand that carrying a third child alone requires more than just checking in on the baby. I wanted them to understand that the stress isn't just about the labor; it's about the invisible labor of protecting her future immune system.

But the reality has been more of a "congratulations, and how are you managing the logistics?" kind of support. They see the physical tasks—the nursery setup, the daycare for the older two, the hospital bag—but they don't see the *mental* weight. When I talk about the microbiome, about wanting to delay that first bath or skip the harsh hospital soap, they smile and say, "Oh, Allegra, you’re just worrying too much. Just get through the birth, the baby will be fine."

They mean well, but it’s dismissive. They treat my research as a symptom of being a single, tired mom rather than a legitimate health pursuit for my daughter. I’ve stopped talking about the "seeding" process with them because I’m tired of the pitying looks. I feel like I’m standing in a room full of people, but I’m the only one who sees the microscopic reality I’m so focused on. It’s a very solitary kind of motherhood.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I really expected, or perhaps just hoped for, someone to sit with me—a friend who’d look past the birth plan and just validate the *why*. I wanted someone to say, "I see you’re trying to protect her," instead of "Just listen to the doctor." I guess I expected my community to hold the space for my anxieties, but instead, they’ve tried to "solve" them by telling me to just stop worrying. It’s almost like they think my concern is a bug to be swatted, rather than a part of how I mother.

What I didn't expect, though, was how much I’ve had to rely on strangers in online forums—people I’ll never meet who are obsessing over the same biological details as I am. It’s strange, right? My real-life friends are worried about nursery colors and diapers, and here I am, sharing tips on microbial transfer with a woman in another state. 

It’s ironic. The support I needed from my local community—people to hold my hand, people to validate my choice to skip the hospital soap—never showed up. Instead, I’m finding this weird, digital comfort from people who understand the science but can’t physically help me navigate the hospital ward. I’m grateful for them, but it highlights just how isolated I am in my own home. I’m surrounded by people who love me, but they don’t see this invisible world I’m building for her, and that hurts more than the back pain.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I approached my birth plan with such hope, really. I typed it up—not as a list of demands, but as a roadmap for the kind of experience I wanted to cultivate for my daughter. I thought it would be a bridge between me and my care team, a way to start a conversation about what matters: skin-to-skin, delayed bathing, immediate contact to foster that microbial transfer. I truly expected them to say, "Okay, let's see how we can make this work."

In reality, my birth plan felt more like a piece of paper they could just check off or, more likely, ignore. During my labor, the nurses were kind, but their primary directive was the monitor. Every time I tried to nudge the conversation toward my preferences, I felt this polite but firm pressure to just go with the flow of the hospital’s standard procedure. They kept pushing the standard interventions, the ones that felt like they were actively stripping away the natural, healthy environment I’d fought so hard to protect for her.

I felt like I was holding that paper like a shield, but the staff was just maneuvering around it. By the time it was all over, it felt like my wishes were treated as "nice-to-haves" rather than core components of her health. It was incredibly disempowering to feel like my autonomy was just an obstacle to their efficiency.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It was the hour right after she was born. My plan was so clear—immediate, uninterrupted skin-to-skin contact, no cold wipes, no rush to the warmer, and definitely no immediate bath. I wanted those hours to be just us, a raw, silent handoff of my biology to hers. But the moment she arrived, there was this flurry of activity.

One of the nurses, very kindly but with zero hesitation, said, "We’re just going to pop her under the warmer for a quick check." It wasn't a question; it was a transition of power. My voice felt so small against the hum of the monitors and the sterile, bright lights. I tried to speak up, saying, "Wait, she’s warm, let’s keep her here," but they were already moving. 

It was like watching a slow-motion car crash. She was whisked away, placed on that cold, sterile metal, and I felt the distance grow instantly. It felt like they were scrubbing away the chance for that microbial transfer I’d spent months worrying about. When they finally brought her back, she smelled like hospital soap, not like *me*. I remember feeling this wave of anger and helplessness. I wasn't just a mother protecting her child; I was a customer being told how the hospital chooses to handle the product. That moment—that stolen hour—still feels like a failure I’m going to have to make up for in the years to come.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I expected a real conversation about it. I went in thinking that we would look at the full menu—not just the "epidural or nothing" binary that everyone assumes. I wanted to talk about how different interventions might affect her, the baby, or even just my own ability to be present for that first, crucial transfer. I thought that by explaining my focus on the microbiome, the team would help me navigate the pain in a way that preserved my autonomy.

The reality was much more reductive. The moment the contractions picked up, the question wasn't, "How are you coping?" or "What do you need to feel centered?" It was, "Are you ready for the epidural yet?" It felt less like a choice and more like a pressure-filled sales pitch. 

It was as if they couldn't conceive of a woman choosing to endure the pain for the sake of the process. Every time I said "no," they’d come back with a tone that suggested I was just making things harder for everyone, including myself. They didn’t see me as a mother making a complex, informed decision; they saw me as a problem to be solved with a needle. By the end, I felt like my resistance to the standard pain management was being treated as a personal affront to their efficiency. It was demoralizing.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I desperately wanted to be able to move freely—to labor in the water, to pace the room, to use gravity to help her descend. I thought that kind of movement would not only help manage the pain, but also keep the environment natural. But every time I tried to move, someone would come in and remind me that the continuous fetal monitoring made movement difficult. I felt like I was physically tethered, not just to the monitor, but to the entire hospital protocol.

When I asked if we could switch to intermittent monitoring—something I’d researched and believed was standard for low-risk cases—I was told, "This is just how we do things here." It was that phrase again. It’s like a brick wall. 

The biggest thing that got in the way wasn't the pain itself; it was the lack of *space*. The room was filled with equipment, wires, and people who seemed determined to keep me in one position—on my back, in the bed, exactly where I didn't want to be. I felt like I was losing my daughter's first hours to a machine. If I could have just had a quiet, dim room where I could sway and move as my body needed, I know I would have felt more empowered. Instead, I felt like a patient who had lost all agency over her own body's mechanics.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I expected the birth setting to feel like a threshold—a sacred space where, even if it were a hospital, the staff would understand the weight of the moment. I thought there would be a sense of being "held" as I transitioned from the outside world into this new, microscopic beginning. I envisioned dim lights, perhaps some quiet, and a team that recognized they were guests in the most important event of my life.

The reality was clinical, jarring, and loud. The moment I walked through the sliding glass doors, I felt like I was being processed by a machine. The fluorescent lights were so harsh they felt like an intrusion; the air was cold and smelled of sharp, synthetic chemicals. I wasn't welcomed as a woman bringing a new life into the world; I was processed as a patient number. Everything moved with this sterile, frantic pace—the squeaky wheels of carts, the beeping of monitors that never seemed to stop, and the endless "how are you feeling" questions that felt more like data collection than genuine concern. 

It was the complete opposite of the calm, natural environment I had built in my mind. It felt hostile to the very idea of a gentle, biological start. I felt tiny, exposed, and entirely out of place in that sea of sterile white.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise—and this is almost impossible to articulate without sounding like I’m making it up—was the sheer, relentless *noise*. Before I arrived, I think I imagined a kind of hushed, focused energy, like a place where a monumental event was unfolding in quiet reverence. I thought there would be a sanctuary-like quality to the room where I’d spend those hours.

Instead, the atmosphere felt like an airport terminal at rush hour. There were alarms from other rooms filtering through the walls, the constant, aggressive clicking of keyboards, and the metallic clatter of equipment being pushed down the hallway. It was sensory overload. Every time a nurse checked a monitor, there was this sharp, clinical sound that felt like an interruption to my body’s rhythm.

I hadn’t realized how much the *soundscape* of a hospital would work against my ability to stay centered. I wanted to focus on my daughter—to sink into that "seeding" process, to feel the rhythm of my own body—but the environment was designed to pull me out of myself and into their system of maintenance and surveillance. It felt like I was trying to hold a private conversation in the middle of a construction site. It wasn't a place for birth; it was a place for processing, and that realization made me feel incredibly, profoundly alone.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I expected a conversation—a pause, even if a brief one, where a provider would say, "Here is the shift, here is why we’re concerned, and here is how we’re thinking about intervening." I thought that even in a crisis, I would be treated as an intelligent adult who had a stake in her own body and her daughter’s future health.

But when the intervention happened—the artificial rupture of membranes, which they framed as "speeding things up"—it felt like a fait accompli. It wasn't a choice; it was a directive. The provider came in, told me it was "time to move things along," and before I could even process what that meant for the natural progression I was hoping for, it was done. There was no discussion of alternatives, no acknowledgment that this could change the entire microbial trajectory of my daughter’s arrival.

It was communicated with a kind of clinical confidence that left no room for "no." They held all the cards, and I was just the person they were doing *to*. I felt completely sidelined. It’s hard to describe the feeling of having your autonomy stripped away in the middle of your own birth, told it’s "for your own good," while you’re physically and emotionally at your most vulnerable. It wasn't a partnership; it was a takeover.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

There was absolutely no sense of choice. It felt like I was an object being serviced on an assembly line. When they decided to augment my labor—when they broke my water without a real conversation—it felt like they were just ticking a box on a chart to move the "case" along to the next phase.

I didn’t feel like I had sufficient information. I was never told, "This will increase the risk of infection," or "This will change the bacterial environment she’s born into." I was just told, "This is necessary," as if it were a scientific fact rather than a choice made by someone with a preference for speed. In those moments, I wasn't a partner; I was a passenger in a vehicle I didn't know how to steer, and the driver was heading in a direction I explicitly asked not to go.

The most painful part is that I remember feeling like I had to apologize for even *hesitating*. I would look up at them, trying to catch their eye, trying to say, "Wait, what does this mean?" but they were already moving to the next task. It happened *to* me. I was a bystander to my own daughter’s birth. The loss of that agency is something I still carry with me; it’s a wound that doesn’t close just because the baby arrived healthy. I feel like they stole the beginning of her story, and they didn't even notice they were doing it.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I suppose I expected them to act like they were witnesses to a miracle, not just task managers in scrubs. I wanted someone who would catch my eye during the most intense waves, maybe offer a hand or a word that acknowledged that I was doing something sacred. I expected them to be the anchor, you know? Someone who would say, "You're doing this, you're doing so well, and you're keeping her safe."

The reality was clinical detachment. They were perfectly polite, but it felt like a customer service interaction at a high-volume warehouse. They were checking the monitors, adjusting the IV, updating the chart, but they weren't *with* me. Whenever I looked for a connection—for a sense of shared focus—I was met with a glance at a screen or a hurried, "You’re doing great, just keep breathing." It felt like a canned response, like they were reading from a script so they could move on to the next patient.

When I tried to share my fears, or even just my exhaustion, the staff seemed almost uncomfortable. They’d quickly pivot to the clinical data to deflect any emotional weight. It was like they were allergic to the intimacy of the moment. I didn't need a cheerleader; I needed a human who understood that I was scared for my daughter’s future, but all I got was a professional performing a job.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

The moment I felt most profoundly alone was right after the birth, during those initial few hours I’d spent months mentally preparing for. I was lying there in the hospital bed, exhausted, and my daughter was finally in my arms, but the room felt crowded with everything except peace.

I had asked—I’d practically begged—for the lights to be dimmed and for us to have an hour of uninterrupted, quiet bonding. But a nurse came in, flipped the harsh overhead lights back on, and started bustling around, checking equipment and talking loudly about her shift handover. It felt like an intrusion into a sacred space. I remember looking at my daughter, her face so small and vulnerable, and feeling this sudden, sharp panic that the "seeding" process I’d researched was being interrupted by the sterile, antiseptic air they were circulating. 

I looked at the nurse, hoping for even a tiny flicker of recognition—a look that said, "I understand why this is special for you." Instead, she didn't even meet my eyes; she was just adjusting the monitor leads. It was the most isolating experience of my life. I was holding the most important person in the world, yet I felt like a stranger in a system that didn't care about the microscopic foundation I was trying to protect. It wasn't just a moment of neglect; it was a erasure of my agency as her mother.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this vision of a quiet, warm cocoon. I pictured her landing on my chest, still slick and smelling of life, and staying there for that golden hour. I wanted it to be just the two of us, no scales, no tape measures, no "quick check" at the warmer. I wanted that direct transfer of my skin’s flora to hers—her first sensory experience of the world to be *me*, not a sanitized hospital room. I promised her, in those woods back home, that I’d be her shield against that sterile intrusion.

But the reality was a blur of bright lights and cold hands. The second she was out, there was this surge of activity, like they were desperate to get her "processed." They swaddled her in a pre-warmed blanket that smelled like industrial laundry detergent, not like me. Even the skin-to-skin felt performative, something they checked off a list while already moving on to the next task. They didn't see a biological handoff; they saw a newborn to be weighed and tagged. 

I felt this frantic, clawing need to pull her back into me, to hide her from the antiseptic air, but I was so drained. They took her away for those "routine" checks, and in that vacuum, I felt like I’d failed the one promise I’d made her. That window for her immune system’s first, natural, essential start? It felt like it had been permanently slammed shut. I didn't get my golden hour; I got a clinical workflow.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It’s the smell. That sounds strange, I know, but it haunts me. I had imagined her first breath—that first "seeding"—would be full of the unique, complex scent of my skin, the culmination of everything I’d built inside me. I wanted her to take in the microbes that were supposed to be her lifelong shield. 

Instead, the very first thing she was wrapped in—this heavy, stifling, pre-warmed hospital blanket—carried that sharp, sterile, chemical scent of industrial laundry detergent. It was everywhere. It felt like they were suffocating her first sensory experience with the smell of a sanitized ward. Every time I see a picture of her from those first few hours, I don’t just see her face; I can practically smell that antiseptic, synthetic, cold blanket. 

It makes me feel like I didn’t just lose a moment; I lost the chance to give her the baseline I’d promised. I still wonder, even now, if that initial exposure to those harsh chemicals, that lack of my own natural flora during those first, most vulnerable seconds, has somehow changed her. It feels like a stolen beginning, and every time I hold her, I’m still trying to apologize to her for it. I was supposed to be her protector, and in those seconds, I felt completely overpowered by the system.

Sometimes, I catch myself looking at her—at her little hands, the way she breathes when she sleeps—and I feel this sharp, sudden ache. It’s not just the new-mother exhaustion. It’s this lingering, persistent grief over that first hour. I know people would tell me I’m being dramatic, that she’s healthy and safe and that’s all that matters. But they don't understand that for me, the *how* was the "why." 

I spent those months in the woods preparing her for a natural, microbial welcome, and instead, I watched her be processed like a piece of cargo on a conveyor belt. It makes me feel like I’m constantly running a race I’ve already lost. Every time she has a sniffle or a skin breakout, I spiral. I wonder if the diversity of her gut flora is compromised, if that sterile blanket and that antiseptic soap were the first bricks in a wall that will separate her from the immunity I fought so hard to give her.

It’s exhausting to carry that, especially alone. I don’t talk about it much anymore. I’ve learned that people can’t handle hearing that a "healthy" outcome didn't feel like a win to the mother. But I still whisper to her when I’m nursing her in the dark. I try to make up for those lost moments, trying to flood her with me, with my skin, with everything I am, hoping—praying—that it’s enough to rewrite that first, cold chapter. I just wish I hadn't had to spend my motherhood trying to fix a start that should have been hers, and mine, to begin with.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, all the time. It wasn't even a matter of a direct confrontation—it was more like a slow, steady erosion of my voice. The worst was from the staff. I’d be in the middle of a contraction, trying to stay centered, and a nurse would swoop in, adjust my IV, and say, "We need to keep you on your side to keep the monitors happy." 

I didn't ask for her opinion on my posture. I was trying to find a position that helped me labor, but she made it sound like my movements were a threat to the baby's safety. She wasn't just giving clinical advice; she was overriding my body’s wisdom with the machine's requirements. And because I was so vulnerable—so tired and focused on that "seeding" goal—I just complied. 

Even my own mother, who I love dearly, would chime in with, "Just do what they say, Allegra. They’re the experts." She’d say it with such certainty, and it felt like a betrayal. I wanted her to be my ally, someone who saw the *why* behind my choices, but instead, she became just another voice echoing the system’s demand for compliance. It’s a terrible feeling, having everyone around you prioritize the protocol over your own intuition. You start to doubt yourself, wondering if maybe you *are* the one who’s wrong, even when every fiber of your being is telling you otherwise.

It’s funny, the way people treat "birth" as if it’s just the destination—like arriving at a station on a train. But it’s not. It’s the transition itself. That transition *is* the foundation.

I sometimes wonder if I’m grieving something that the world thinks doesn't exist. People talk about the "miracle of life," but they focus on the heartbeats and the healthy Apgar scores. They don't see the unseen—the microscopic ballet that happens when a child meets her mother’s biology for the first time. They think it’s just bacteria, just "stuff" to be cleaned away with soap and sterile, white-towel protocols. They don't see that they were scrubbing away her primary defense system.

I’m thirty-four weeks along with my *third* child—I know what people think of me. They think I’m "that" woman. The one who reads too much, who wants to control the uncontrollable, who shouldn't be so anxious. They don't realize that being a single parent has made me hyper-vigilant because I *have* to be. I am the only one who can stand between her and a world that prioritizes convenience and "sterility" over the messy, ancient reality of biology.

I’m tired of fighting this silent, invisible war. I’m tired of being the only one who cares about the "good bugs." I just wish, for once, that someone in that white coat would have looked at me and said, "I hear you, Allegra. Let’s do this your way, because it’s your daughter, and it’s your body, and it’s your future." 

Is it really so much to ask to be treated like a mother rather than a patient number? I’m terrified that I’ll get to the end of this and realize that the system—so focused on "safety"—has left her just a little bit more vulnerable than she ever should have been. And that is a burden I’m not sure how to put down.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

That's a complicated question, especially being a single parent this time around. But even without a partner in the room, I feel the "ghost" of those societal expectations pressing down on me. It’s like the system assumes that if I’m not following their script, I must be struggling to manage, or that my choices are just the result of having no one to tell me otherwise.

There was a moment during a prenatal check-up when the doctor asked, "And who will be helping you with the transition home?" When I told her it was just me, her expression shifted—it wasn't empathy, it was judgment masked as concern. She immediately started rattling off resources, classes, and "support systems" that were all designed to make me more like the standard patient. It felt like she was telling me my life—my choice to do this alone—was a risk factor, and that I needed to "fix" my lifestyle to be more manageable for *them*.

I felt this intense pressure to align with their idea of a "stable" pregnancy. They treat my independence as a problem. They want me to fit the mold, to be the patient who says "yes" to every intervention, to every test, to every routine. I’m doing this on my own because I know I’m capable, but the medical system is constantly pushing back, suggesting that without a partner or a "normal" family structure, my decisions aren't as valid. It's exhausting, having to defend my life choices while I'm also trying to defend my daughter's first breath. I’m tired of being the only one holding the line.

I find myself thinking about the silence after the baby arrives—not the medical silence, but the internal one. There’s this expectation that after the delivery, you’ll just fold back into your life, that you’ll be the same person you were before. But I’m not. I’m marked by these choices. I feel like I’m constantly looking at her—this tiny, fragile thing—and wondering if the world she’s entering is truly as "safe" as the doctors say.

They call it "standard of care," but that phrase is so sterile. It feels like it’s meant to strip away the *person* and leave only the process. And when I look at the medical system, I don't see a health provider; I see an industrial machine. It wants to sanitize, to measure, to categorize. And I? I’m just trying to keep the humanity in the handoff.

If I could say one thing to those doctors, those nurses—the ones who sighed when I asked for a little more time—it would be this: "You might be doing this every single day, but for us, this is the first and only time it will ever happen. Why is it so hard to see that?" 

I’m 34 weeks now. The woods are quiet, and for a few minutes, when the wind rustles the leaves, I feel like I can actually breathe. I’m going to keep talking to her. I’m going to keep promising her that she’s worth the fight, even if I have to wage it all by myself. I just hope, when the time finally comes, I can hold onto my voice. I don't want to leave the delivery room feeling like I betrayed her. I just want to leave feeling like I gave her the best, most natural start I possibly could.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, absolutely. The comments about my weight have been a constant, nagging refrain. It feels like they look at me and see a "type" of patient before they ever look at my actual health or my daughter’s development. 

Early on, a provider made this offhand remark about my weight gain—not a medical concern, mind you, just a judgmental observation. It landed like a heavy, cold stone in my stomach. I’m carrying a human being, growing her bones and her brain, and all they can focus on is the number on the scale. It felt like they were judging my "discipline," as if my body were failing some invisible standard of aesthetic perfection. 

It makes me feel so exposed. It’s hard enough to feel the physical weight of this pregnancy, the way my body has changed, the way it’s working overtime to build her foundation. To have that struggle reduced to a critique on my diet or my size feels like a profound misunderstanding of what’s happening in this room. It’s another way they distance themselves from the reality of motherhood; they categorize me, critique me, and move on. It makes me want to withdraw, to close myself off, because how can I trust someone to respect the delicate, invisible "seeding" process I’m so worried about when they can’t even look past the superficial, physical numbers on a chart? It’s just one more layer of feeling like I’m being managed, not cared for.

I think what’s most infuriating about those weight comments, or even the subtle jabs about my diet, is how they are delivered with such nonchalant, professional authority. It’s almost as if they think that by pointing out these "issues," they’re being helpful, or even "proactive." They don’t see the way it shrinks me. 

I’m standing there, thirty-four weeks into a pregnancy I’m navigating alone, carrying the weight of a third child and the immense, quiet anxiety of how to best protect her immune system—and they are focused on a centimeter of measurement or a caloric recommendation. It feels like they’re trying to force me to conform to some standardized, "perfect" version of a pregnant woman, and in doing so, they completely ignore the person actually standing in front of them.

It reminds me that, to the system, I’m just a dataset. If I don't fit the curve, I’m an outlier. If I express concern about the microbiome, I’m "that" patient. If I don’t follow their exact weight-gain protocol, I’m "non-compliant." 

I keep coming back to this idea that the system is trying to build a wall between me and my own experience. It’s a wall made of clinical detachment, standardized forms, and unsolicited advice. And every brick—every dismissive sigh, every lecture on my weight, every rush to get me onto a monitor—makes it just that much harder for me to stay present for her. 

I just want to be seen for what I am: a mother, a guardian, and a person who is doing everything in her power to start this life off right. Is that really so much to ask? Or am I just a body to be managed until the moment of delivery? Sometimes, I honestly don't know the answer, and that, in itself, is the hardest thing to hold.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I found myself turning to the digital woods, if that makes sense. It’s these niche, sometimes fringe, online parenting and biological forums. I remember one night, around 2:00 a.m., when the back pain was searing and I was spiraling about the hospital’s C-section rates. I didn't call the on-call line—I knew I’d just be a "bother." Instead, I went to these groups where mothers—strangers, really—share the actual, nitty-gritty science of "seeding" and why that first hour matters.

It felt like coming up for air. For the first time, I wasn't the "anxious, difficult patient"; I was a mother asking valid, scientifically grounded questions. These people didn't sigh. They didn't roll their eyes. They sent me links to research papers, tips on how to word my birth preferences to get the staff to listen, and—most importantly—they validated that my fear wasn't a pathology. It was a maternal instinct.

That informal community became my real "care team." They’ve given me more practical, emotional, and even clinical guidance than the clinic ever has. It’s ironic, isn't it? I have to go to a screen to find the humanity and the science that I should have been able to access in the brick-and-mortar office down the road. They’re the ones who helped me feel like I’m not crazy for wanting her to have my bugs instead of the hospital’s. I trust them more than the people who, on paper, are responsible for my pregnancy. That’s a hard realization to sit with.

It’s strange how clear the line is: the hospital feels like a place where they subtract from the experience—they subtract my choice, my autonomy, my daughter's natural introduction to the world—while the people online, those strangers in the digital void, add to it. They give me the tools to reclaim what is rightfully mine. They’ve helped me draft my birth preferences in a way that sounds "medical enough" to maybe, *just maybe*, get the doctor to pause. It’s sad that I have to use their language, their clinical jargon, just to ask for the most basic, biological right to skin-to-skin contact.

The other night, I was reading about a woman who had a C-section and how she essentially "seeded" her daughter with her own fluids afterward because the hospital wouldn't allow it during the procedure. It sounds so intense to someone outside this, doesn't it? But to me, it felt like an act of profound, protective love. It made me cry. It made me realize that I’m part of this secret, quiet lineage of mothers who know that the "standard of care" isn't the final word on health.

I’m thirty-four weeks along, and the woods are my only quiet space now. I’m preparing for the fight, really. Because that’s what this is. I’m going into that hospital knowing I might have to be the difficult one. I might have to say "no" to a hundred well-meaning but invasive offers. I’m ready to be labeled the "anxious, stubborn mother" if it means I get to protect those first few hours for her. I just hope I’m strong enough to hold that line when the monitors are beeping and the clock is ticking. I don’t want to be a patient; I want to be a mother. And for some reason, in this system, those two things feel like they’re at war.

I think about that war a lot—the one between being a mother and being a patient. It’s almost as if the system is built to erase the mother in favor of the "outcome." They treat the baby like a project, like a medical deliverable, and the mother’s body is just the infrastructure they need to get through. But I’m not just infrastructure. I’m the *source*. 

Every time I feel her kick, I realize she’s learning about the world through me, and that connection is so fragile. When I’m at the hospital, they treat that connection like a potential interference. "Can you move? Can you shift? Can we check the monitor? Can we do this test?" It’s all designed to sever the focus I have on her, to pull me out of that intuitive loop and force me into their operational one. 

I’m terrified that I’ll get to the end and look back and feel like I missed her entrance because I was too busy defending the room. I don’t want to be a combatant; I just want to be a mother. But the system is so rigid that "mothering" in the way I feel is necessary—this protective, biological, instinctual way—is treated as a form of non-compliance. 

It’s hard to stay steady. Sometimes I feel fierce, like I’m ready to stand my ground against a hundred nurses. Other times, I feel like I’m already broken, just another woman who had to trade her voice for a "safe" exit. I’m thirty-four weeks along, and I’m holding onto the hope that, just for a moment, I’ll find someone in that room who sees it the way I do. A doctor who smiles and says, "Take your time. This is yours." If I could find that—if I could just have one person on my side—I think I could finally breathe. Until then, I’ll keep walking these woods and whispering to her, promising her that I’m still here, and I’m still fighting, even if I’m the only one who knows why.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was one moment that broke it, completely and irrevocably. I was about 28 weeks, and I finally worked up the courage to bring in a printed, highlighted research summary on the importance of the maternal microbiome for initial immune system colonization. I wasn't being pushy; I was being careful. I handed it to my doctor, hoping—just praying—she would glance at it and say, "That's fascinating, let's see how we can align our protocols with this."

She didn't even look at the paper. She set it on the desk next to her computer, let out this long, weary sigh, and said, "Allegra, you’re reading too much on the internet again. You need to stop worrying about these 'micro-details' and focus on the big picture: a healthy baby and a smooth delivery. Let us worry about the science; you just focus on relaxing."

That was it. That "relaxing" comment—it was so patronizing, so dismissive of the fact that this *is* the big picture for me. She reduced my daughter’s entire biological foundation to "micro-details." In that second, the trust just evaporated. I realized she didn't see me as an intelligent partner; she saw me as a nuisance who was complicating her schedule with my "fanciful" concerns. It made me realize that I am completely, utterly on my own in that room. I stopped sharing my thoughts after that. I just nod, smile, and wait for the appointment to end, all while keeping the real fight buried inside, waiting for the day I finally have to protect my daughter from their "standard of care."

That sigh—that weariness—was the moment I became a closed book. It’s hard to describe the coldness that settles over you when you realize you aren't being treated as a teammate, but as an annoyance. I realized then that my concern for her immunity, for that delicate "seeding" of her gut, was something they were only going to tolerate if I kept it quiet and didn’t challenge their routine.

So, I started playing the part. I walk in, I wear the hospital-approved smile, I nod when they talk about "standard procedures," and I wait for the Doppler check to be over. It’s a performative kind of pregnancy. Inside, though, I’m screaming. I’m thinking about every single thing I’ve read, every peer-reviewed study, every account of birth trauma where a mother’s voice was silenced by a "just relax" or a "we’ve got this."

I’m thirty-four weeks now, and the woods here are my only real sanctuary. I don’t feel like I’m in a clinical program; I feel like I’m in a minefield. I’m waiting for the big day, knowing that my birth plan is just a piece of paper that’s likely to be ignored, and that I’ll have to be a guard at the door of my own labor. It’s such a heavy, lonely burden to realize that the people whose job it is to bring my daughter into the world are the very people I have to shield her from. I just hope I’m strong enough. I’m not doing this for the system; I’m doing this for her. And if I have to be the difficult, loud, "internet-obsessed" mother to make sure she gets her first coat of good bacteria, then that’s exactly who I’m going to be.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

It’s not just the absence of a partner; it’s the way the system is designed to highlight that empty chair. Every time I sit in those exam rooms and the doctor asks, "Where's your support person today?" or "We should really have your partner here for this discussion," it’s like a fresh sting. They don't mean to be cruel, but they treat my status as a single parent like a missing piece of equipment—something that *should* be there to help them manage *me*.

There was one scan where the technician was so frustrated that I didn’t have someone else to help hold the photos or interpret the screen. It felt like I was being blamed for the logistics of my own life. I’m there, I’m present, I’m the one carrying her, but to them, I’m "unsupported." They don't see that I’ve built my own support—my own research, my own resolve, my own quiet conversations with her in the woods. 

That isolation is so thick in the room. When I have to make a choice—like when they wanted to schedule a non-stress test—I have to be the one to weigh it, decide it, and face the consequences of saying "no" or "yes" without a second pair of ears to back me up. It’s heavy. It makes the hospital environment feel even more like a place where I’m being sized up and found wanting. I’m doing this all on my own, and it feels like the system is constantly reminding me of that, rather than asking me what I need to feel strong. It’s just one more way I feel like an outlier in their assembly line.

It makes me wonder why they don’t just ask, "How can we help you feel empowered today?" instead of, "Where is your partner?" 

They treat my single-parent status like a deficiency in the care plan, a gap they need to fill with more appointments or more medical oversight. But I’m not deficient. I’m focused. I’m the one who wakes up at 3:00 a.m. to read the latest studies on neonatal gut colonization. I’m the one who walks the woods, talking to her about the world she’s entering. I’m the one holding all the threads, and honestly, that makes me more informed and more present than someone who just shows up to hold a hand and wait for instructions.

The system is so obsessed with the "ideal" nuclear family model that it doesn't know how to handle a woman who is doing this on her own terms. It’s like they assume that if I don’t have a partner, I must be lost or overwhelmed. Maybe I am overwhelmed, but it’s not because I’m alone—it’s because I’m carrying the entire weight of her future health on my own shoulders, and I’m doing it in a system that is constantly pushing back.

I’m thirty-four weeks now, and I’ve stopped looking for that supportive partner in the clinic. I’ve started looking for my own strength. I know that when I walk into that delivery room, I’m going to be the only advocate she has. And that’s okay. It’s a lot of weight, but it’s my weight. I just wish I didn't feel like I had to fight the people who are supposed to be helping me in order to do it. It’s a strange, lonely kind of bravery, but it’s mine. I’ll make sure she gets her "good bugs," even if I have to stand in the middle of that sterile, beeping room and shout it from the rooftops. She’s worth every bit of the fight.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single day, I go online because I can’t get the "why" from my care providers. When I ask them about the microbiome, I get a pamphlet. When I ask about the long-term immunological impact of a sterile birth environment, I get a "don't worry your pretty little head." They treat health as the absence of a crisis, but to me, health is the *presence* of a strong foundation.

I was looking for the nuances that they seem to think don't exist. I was hunting for specific data on how delayed cord clamping and immediate skin-to-skin contact actually work to buffer the baby’s immune system after a birth that is, by definition, an intense, life-altering event. I needed to know the mechanics—how the bacteria transfer, how the skin regulates temperature, how the hormonal cocktail of labor acts as a signal for the baby’s body to start processing these things. 

They won’t give me that. They view me as a person who needs to be managed, not a person who needs to understand. Going online—finding those papers, reading the research, connecting with other mothers who *refuse* to accept that "healthy baby" is the only metric of success—that’s the only way I can reclaim my agency. I’m looking for the truth of the process, the biology of the transition. They treat me like I’m a student who doesn’t need to see the syllabus, just follow the instructions. I don’t want to be a follower; I want to be an informed partner. And since they won't let me into the fold, I had to build my own bridge to the information myself. It’s an exhausting way to prepare for birth, but it’s the only way I can face it.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s this one thing I keep coming back to: the idea that my anxiety—this constant, gnawing worry about her microbiome and her first hour—is viewed by everyone as a *symptom* of being a single mother, or perhaps just a personality flaw. They look at me and see someone who is "high-strung" or "difficult." 

But it’s not a flaw. It’s a form of labor. 

Being a single parent, I don't have the luxury of passing off the mental load to a partner who can handle the hospital staff while I focus on the contractions. I have to be the one who does the research, the one who stays awake in the middle of the night learning about how her gut flora is seeded, and the one who has to be ready to advocate for her the second she’s born. My "anxiety" is just my way of trying to be the village for her when the actual village is just a cold, sterile room full of people who want to rush through the process. 

I just want the maternity care system to understand that when a mother asks questions—even the ones they find "fanciful"—she’s not being difficult. She’s being a mother. We are the ones who will live with the consequences of these first hours for the rest of our lives. I’m thirty-four weeks along, and I’m tired, but I’m ready. I just wish I didn't feel like I was walking into the delivery room as a defender, rather than a mother. I hope that one day, the system learns to listen to the *why* behind the worry, because that’s where the true care actually lives.
